reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper makes two contributions: First, it presents a large-scale 2D fluid simulation dataset called EAGLE. The dataset consists of 2D scenes with (dynamically changing) rigid boundaries and Reynold-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) fluids. Second, it proposes a neural network model (mesh transformer) for solving fluid problems and compares its performance with popular neural network simulators (MeshGraphNet, etc).

Strength And Weaknesses:
The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is very timely. I believe having a high-quality dataset will foster relevant research in the learning community.

On the other hand, part of me feels this dataset is still premature. It is probably better than many existing fluid simulation datasets in previous papers, but I think it could have been more polished.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
There are quite a few things unclear to me in the simulation dataset:
- Are there any reasons behind the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations in the dataset?
- It looks like the drone and the fluid are one-way coupled instead of two-way coupled: the drone provides boundary conditions to the fluid’s motion, but the fluid does not affect the drone’s motion (there are no velocity or pressure terms in Eqn. 8). If the fluid has no influence on the drone, I feel the MPC control is a bit redundant and a kinematic drone should suffice.
- I am not sure I get how the drone’s mesh serves as the boundary condition of the fluid area. It looks like its bounding box is used. Could you elaborate on the velocity/pressure boundary conditions around the bounding boxes? I was expecting some inlet/outlet flow velocity to be set at where the propellers are, but I didn’t find details about it.
- I don’t quite understand the task definition at the end of Sec. 3. Do the node positions x_i change over time? It looks like the fluid area is remeshed at every frame if I am not mistaken.

I initially thought the proposed dataset simulated two-way coupled Navier-Stokes fluids and a dynamic, controllable UAV when reading the main text and was quite excited. It would be good if the paper could clarify the setting upfront in Sec. 3.

Regarding the network model, it looks like the velocity and pressure fields are predicted purely based on the original mesh at the current time followed by resampled to the new mesh if I am not mistaken. This seems questionable to me because I think the correct velocity and pressure field at the next time step will be determined by both the current and new mesh.

Reproducibility: I guess this largely depends on what will be included in the released dataset. Replicating the full simulation results using Ansys is probably intractable for people with limited access to computational resources, but I still suggest the dataset include configurations of the scenes in Ansys if possible.

Other comments:
- Is EAGLE an acronym?
- The baselines are confusing: the beginning of Sec. 5 refers MeshGraphNet and Cylinder-Flow to Li et al. 2019, but I don’t think that paper means either of them. By MeshGraphNet the paper probably means Pfaff et al. [2021].
- I appreciate that the paper frankly talked about failure cases and poor results (Scalar flow) in the experiments.

Summary Of The Review:
Currently, I am leaning toward rejection, although my score is not final yet. It is definitely a respectable submission, and I welcome different opinions from the authors and other reviewers.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The proposal presents a large-scale fluid simulation dataset, which is timely and potentially beneficial for the research community. However, upon careful examination, several concerns and unclear aspects arise that need to be addressed.

**Strengths:**

* The proposal of a large-scale fluid simulation dataset is well-timed and has the potential to significantly benefit the research community.
* The authors are commended for frankly discussing the failure cases and poor results in the experiments, demonstrating a high level of transparency and honesty.

**Weaknesses and Suggestions:**

* The dataset could have been more polished, which raises concerns about its current state and usability.
* Several aspects of the simulation dataset are unclear, particularly the choice of simulating RANS instead of the original Navier-Stokes equations. A clear justification for this choice is necessary.
* The task definition at the end of Section 3 lacks clarity, making it difficult to understand the specific objectives and scope of the project.
* The mesh transformer model seems questionable, as it predicts velocity and pressure fields based on the original mesh at the current time. This approach may not be the most effective or accurate way to model fluid dynamics.

**Main Concerns:**

* The reviewer is concerned that the dataset is still premature and could benefit from further refinement.
* The understanding is that the dataset simulates one-way coupled fluids and UAV motion, which might limit its realism and applicability.
* The reviewer questions the mesh transformer model's approach to predicting velocity and pressure fields, which may not accurately capture the complex dynamics of fluid flows.

**Technical Clarifications:**

* The choice of RANS over Navier-Stokes equations for the simulation is unclear and requires justification.
* The role of the drone's mesh in the fluid area boundary conditions is not well-defined and needs clarification.
* It is unclear whether the node positions change over time and how the fluid area is remeshed, which is crucial for understanding the simulation's accuracy and reliability.

**Recommendations:**

* Clarify the setting upfront in Section 3, particularly regarding the type of fluid simulation and UAV control.
* Include configurations of the scenes in Ansys in the released dataset for reproducibility.
* Provide a clear definition of the task and the role of the drone's mesh in the fluid area boundary conditions.

**Conclusion:**

While the proposal presents an interesting and potentially valuable contribution to the research community, several concerns and unclear aspects need to be addressed. The reviewer believes that with significant revisions to address these concerns, the proposal could be reconsidered for acceptance. However, in its current state, the proposal falls short of the acceptance threshold.

**Rating:** 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)

The revisions and clarifications requested are necessary to bring the proposal up to the required standards, and it is hoped that the authors will address these concerns in their future work.

title: Consolidator: Mergable Adapter with Group Connections for Visual Adaptation

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a consolidator that modifies the pretrained model with the addition of a small set of tunable parameters to store the task-specific knowledge while freezing the backbone model during adaptation. A two-stage consolidation process is designed by merging corresponding parameters in the training-storage phase and loading-inference phase. Extensive experiments are conducted on various downstream tasks and the results outperform state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

(1) The authors propose a basic module, a consolidator for efficient transfer learning.

(2) A two-stage consolidation process is designed. 

(3) Extensive experiments on various downstream tasks show state-of-the-art performance.

Weaknesses:
This work aims to design a mergeable adapter for efficient transfer learning and proposes a two-stage consolidation process by merging corresponding parameters. I have some questions as follows. 

(1)  In Figure 1, the authors split channels and use zero padding, which might cause a heavy computational cost. Thus, the training time/speed should be shown for comparison with other tuning methods. Besides, I notice that the authors insert a consolidator in the FC layer in Figure 1. Will performance be further enhanced if the consolidator is inserted in other locations? Some recent tuning methods [1, 2] should also be discussed and compared in Table 1. 

(2)  Some other tuning baselines are missing in Table 4. The authors should conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons. 

(3)  The consolidator is designed for a linear layer. Can it be applied to other operational structures, such as self attention. Further, the current consolidator module is designed mainly in the vision transformer. In Table 1, Table 4 and Table 5, the proposed method obtains promising performance compared to the Full tuning. Push it forward, can it be extended to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures [3,4,5]? These will be interesting experiments. Due to the rebuttal time constraints, the authors do not need to add these extension experiments, but feel free to discuss them. 

(4)  There exists a large number of typos in the current version, the authors are able to polish it further. 

[1] ‘AdaptFormer: Adapting Vision Transformers for Scalable Visual Recognition’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[2] ‘Scaling & Shifting Your Features: A New Baseline for Efficient Model Tuning’, in NeurIPS2022. 

[3] ‘A ConvNet for the 2020s’, in CVPR2022.

[4] ‘AS-MLP: An Axial Shifted MLP Architecture for Vision’, in ICLR2022.

[5] ‘RepMLPNet: Hierarchical Vision MLP with Re-parameterized Locality’, in CVPR2022.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: It should not be hard to reproduce this work because the authors describe the specific details for training and hyper parameters. The authors also propose consolidation process, which is also original in efficient transfer learning.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This work proposes a two-stage consolidation process for efficient transfer learning. It outperforms state-of-the-art methods with fewer stored parameters but superior performance. I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes a consolidator for efficient transfer learning, which is a timely and interesting contribution to the field. The authors have designed a basic module, a consolidator, and a two-stage consolidation process that enables efficient transfer learning. The extensive experiments on various downstream tasks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, outperforming other methods with fewer stored parameters.

One of the key strengths of this paper is the proposal of a consolidator for efficient transfer learning. The authors have made a good effort in designing a two-stage consolidation process, which involves merging corresponding parameters in the training-storage phase and loading-inference phase. The experimental results show that the consolidator achieves state-of-the-art performance on various downstream tasks.

However, there are some weaknesses that need to be addressed. Firstly, the authors should provide a comparison of the training time/speed with other tuning methods. This would help to evaluate the efficiency of the proposed consolidator. Additionally, the consolidator is currently designed for a linear layer and is inserted in the FC layer. It would be interesting to explore whether the performance can be enhanced if it is inserted in other locations or applied to other operational structures, such as self-attention.

Furthermore, the authors should discuss and compare recent tuning methods in Table 1. Some recent methods are missing, and a more comprehensive comparison would strengthen the paper. Moreover, the authors should conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons in Table 4. This would help to demonstrate the superiority of the proposed consolidator.

The authors also mention that the current consolidator module is designed mainly in the vision transformer, and it would be interesting to explore whether it can be extended to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures. This could be a potential direction for future work.

To further improve the paper, I suggest that the authors polish the manuscript to improve clarity and reduce typos. Additionally, they should address the above-mentioned weaknesses and suggestions to strengthen the paper.

Overall, I believe that the paper has a good idea and makes a valuable contribution to the field. With some additional experiments and revisions, the paper can be further improved. Therefore, I recommend that the paper be accepted with a rating of 8, as it is a good paper that is marginally above the acceptance threshold.

**Rating: 8 (Accept)**

**Recommendations:**

* Provide a comparison of the training time/speed with other tuning methods.
* Discuss and compare recent tuning methods in Table 1.
* Conduct more experiments with other tuning methods for fair comparisons in Table 4.
* Explore the application of the consolidator to other operational structures, such as self-attention.
* Extend the consolidator to other model families such as CNNs or MLP architectures.
* Polish the manuscript to improve clarity and reduce typos.

I tend to vote marginally above the acceptance threshold. Some additional experiments should be conducted to further improve the quality of this manuscript.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper

reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
#### Strength

3. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.

#### Weaknesses

1. The novelty of this work is limited. The mergeable design is proposed by LoRA [1] for efficient parameter finetuing. Besides, what's the main difference between the **Channel Reorder** and **shuffle** operator in ShuffleNet[2]?

2. Some recent related works are not discussed, e.g. [3][4][5].

reviews.text:
This review assesses the paper proposing a 'consolidator' for efficient fine-tuning of transformers, offering a novel approach to parameter efficiency. 

The paper presents a well-structured and easy-to-follow proposal for a consolidator that enables efficient fine-tuning of transformers. The consolidator's multi-branch structure can be merged into a single matrix, allowing for parameter efficiency. The two-stage consolidation process is a notable aspect of this work, maximizing adaptation capacity without incurring additional inference costs.

However, upon closer examination, it becomes apparent that the novelty of the proposed approach is limited. The mergeable design bears a strong resemblance to previously proposed methods, such as LoRA. Furthermore, the distinction between the 'Channel Reorder' operation and the 'shuffle' operator in ShuffleNet remains unclear. These aspects detract from the paper's overall impact.

The paper could be strengthened by engaging with recent related works, such as [3], [4], and [5], to better situate the consolidator within the existing landscape of transformer fine-tuning methods. Additionally, clarifying the differences between 'Channel Reorder' and 'shuffle' operator would enhance the paper's clarity and contribute to a more robust understanding of the consolidator's advantages.

While the results presented demonstrate some improvement over existing methods, such as NOAH, the marginal gains raise questions about the consolidator's overall effectiveness. The improvement, as shown in Table 1, is not substantial, which may limit the paper's impact.

In conclusion, while the paper presents an interesting approach to efficient transformer fine-tuning, its limitations in novelty and the marginal improvement over existing methods mean that it falls short of being a strong contribution. Therefore, I recommend that this paper is not accepted in its current form, which aligns with a rating of 5, indicating that it is marginally below the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a new fine-tuning method for vision transformers that only trains and stores very few parameters. The fine-tuning method achieves both efficiency and effectiveness through its design of mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting. Extensive experiments show the method surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning on several transfer learning tasks.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Consolidator is a very effective method. Compared with other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning methods, Consolidator can achieve an almost optimal transfer learning effect.
2. Compared to the adapter methods, the design in this paper can be merged with the original model so that the inference phase does not increase the cost of memory and computation time.

Weakness:
1. The prior of ChannelReorder operation is strong but the explanation of its effectiveness is weak. 
2. The parameters in all tables are stored parameters for Consolidator but not trainable parameters.
3. It should be possible to save time compared to the full parameter finetune during training, I wonder why this is not reported, nor is it compared to other baseline training time.
4. The value of saving storage space is not so great, especially for the base-level model, and there are few experiments on the Large model.
5. There are slightly more spelling errors and grammatical errors. P3 workss; P4 ti, thw; P5 the all the; P7 accomodate.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper presents a new fine-tuning method for vision transformers that only trains and stores very few parameters. The fine-tuning method achieves both efficiency and effectiveness through its design of mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting. Extensive experiments show the method surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning on several transfer learning tasks.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
1. Consolidator is a very effective method. Compared with other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning methods, Consolidator can achieve an almost optimal transfer learning effect.
2. Compared to the adapter methods, the design in this paper can be merged with the original model so that the inference phase does not increase the cost of memory and computation time.

Weakness:
1. The prior of ChannelReorder operation is strong but the explanation of its effectiveness is weak. 
2. The parameters in all tables are stored parameters for Consolidator but not trainable parameters.
3. It should be possible to save time compared to the full parameter finetune during training, I wonder why this is not reported, nor is it compared to other baseline training time.
4. The value of saving storage space is not so great, especially for the base-level model, and there are few experiments on the Large model.
5. There are slightly more spelling errors and grammatical errors. P3 workss; P4 ti, thw; P5 the all the; P7 accomodate.

See Strength And Weaknesses

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper presents a novel fine-tuning method called Consolidator, designed for vision transformers, which selectively trains and stores only a few parameters. This approach aims to achieve a balance between effectiveness and efficiency in transfer learning tasks.

**Strengths:**

The Consolidator method demonstrates remarkable effectiveness, achieving near-optimal transfer learning performance compared to other adapter methods and full parameter fine-tuning approaches. A significant advantage of Consolidator is its design, which allows it to merge with the original model. This integration ensures that the inference phase does not incur additional memory or computation time costs, making it a highly efficient solution.

**Weaknesses and Areas for Improvement:**

Despite its strengths, the paper has several areas that require clarification and improvement. Firstly, the prior of the ChannelReorder operation is strong; however, the explanation of its effectiveness is somewhat weak. Further elaboration on this aspect would enhance the paper's comprehensibility and robustness. Additionally, there seems to be a lack of clarity in the presentation of parameters in the tables, which are stated to represent stored parameters for Consolidator, not trainable parameters. This distinction is crucial for a thorough understanding of the method's efficiency.

Moreover, while Consolidator is expected to save time compared to full parameter fine-tuning during training, the paper does not report or compare training times with other baseline methods. This omission is significant, as it would provide a more comprehensive view of Consolidator's efficiency. Furthermore, the value of saving storage space is not substantial, particularly for the base-level model. The paper could benefit from more experiments on larger models, such as the Large model, to demonstrate the scalability and applicability of Consolidator across different model sizes.

Lastly, there are some spelling and grammatical errors throughout the paper, which slightly detract from its overall clarity and professionalism.

**Main Points and Technical Assessment:**

The Consolidator method's design, featuring mergeable multiple branches and parameter shifting, enables it to achieve both efficiency and effectiveness. The paper presents evidence that Consolidator surpasses other adapter methods and full fine-tuning approaches on several transfer learning tasks, highlighting its potential as a viable fine-tuning strategy.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To strengthen the paper, I recommend the following:

1. **Provide a stronger explanation for the effectiveness of the ChannelReorder operation.** This would help in understanding the underlying mechanisms of Consolidator and enhance its credibility.
2. **Report and compare training times with other baseline methods.** This comparison is essential for evaluating Consolidator's efficiency during the training phase.
3. **Conduct more experiments on larger models.** This would provide insights into Consolidator's performance and scalability across different model sizes.
4. **Improve the clarity of the paper by reducing spelling and grammatical errors.** Enhancing the paper's readability and professionalism is crucial for effective communication of its contributions.

**Overall Assessment:**

While the Consolidator method shows promise in achieving a balance between effectiveness and efficiency in fine-tuning vision transformers, the paper falls marginally below the acceptance threshold due to the identified weaknesses and areas for improvement. Addressing these concerns would significantly enhance the paper's quality and contribution to the field.

**Decision:** 5 - marginally below the acceptance threshold

In conclusion, with revisions addressing the mentioned weaknesses and suggestions, the paper has the potential to make a more substantial impact in the field of transfer learning and vision transformers.

The main concerns are listed below. 
- Strictly restricted application scenario. The author introduces the application scenario in the abstract where the resource-limited devices cannot store a full copy of parameters but could provide computation to train/fine-tune the large models. It is a bit strange the devices with the ability to train DL models do not have sufficient storage.

- Some recent literature on the adaptation of Transformers is missing.

reviews.text:
This review assesses the submission with a rating of 5, indicating that it falls marginally below the acceptance threshold. The paper explores the adaptation of well-trained transformers to downstream tasks on resource-limited devices, proposing a method that utilizes grouped connections with re-parameterization technology. 

The authors demonstrate improvements in accuracy and computation cost compared to baseline methods, which is a significant strength. Additionally, the inclusion of ablation experiments helps validate the effectiveness of their proposed approach. These aspects contribute positively to the paper's overall quality.

However, several limitations detract from the paper's impact. A major concern is the restricted application scenario, which may not offer practical value to a broad audience. This limitation is crucial as it restricts the applicability and generalizability of the proposed method. Furthermore, the paper could benefit from a discussion on the relationship between the proposed method and re-parameterization technology, as well as an overview of recent literature on adapting Transformers. The omission of these aspects leaves the impression that the paper is not fully developed.

To strengthen the paper, the authors should consider revising it to address these concerns. They should explore more practical application scenarios to enhance the method's usability and relevance. Moreover, including a review of recent literature on Transformer adaptation and clarifying the relationship between their method and existing re-parameterization techniques would be beneficial. This would not only improve the paper's depth but also situate the proposed method within the broader context of the field.

In conclusion, while the paper presents some promising ideas and preliminary results, it falls short of being accepted in its current form. The revisions suggested above are crucial for enhancing the paper's relevance, depth, and overall contribution to the field. Therefore, I recommend that the authors undertake these revisions to strengthen their submission. 

The rating of 5 reflects the paper's potential but also its current limitations and the need for further development to meet the standards of the field.

title: Equal Improvability: A New Fairness Notion Considering the Long-term Impact

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments. 

--- post rebuttal --- 
I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This work proposes a notion of long-term impact called equal improvability (EI) that equalizes the "effort required to improve" of rejected individuals belonging to different sensitive groups. This metric is theoretically compared to three other long-term metrics: Bounded Effort and Equal Recourse---conditions for meeting each of these notions of fairness (in relation to each other) are derived. Three fairness-based regularization methods are proposed to find policies that mitigate unfairness wrt EI, each with a unique differentiable penalty term. These methods are empirically tested against synthetic and real-world data. In the first set of experiments, the three mitigation strategies are tested against a fairness-unaware algorithm over two real-world datasets (non-dynamic). In the second set of experiments, results for a simple dynamical setting are shown.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths / comments: 
- I enjoyed reading the introduction and the (first) related work section, where the authors pinpoint the importance of their metric and show how it is distinct from current known metrics in the field. 
- Concepts are elaborated on in the appendix or figures are provided to help readers better understand material. The example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in the para underneath Defn 2.1 was very helpful. 
- The authors did not over-state the results of their fairness-based regularization algorithms, e.g., acknowledged that these methods can mitigate EI unfairness and do not provide further guarantees on solutions found. 
- The organization of section 3 was straightforward and well-written. 

Weaknesses / comments: 

- Why don't the authors compare the other long-term fairness metrics to the long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments? ERM is a very simple baseline, and arguably the three datasets provided are three more datasets over which other long-term fairness baselines can be used as a comparison. We could consider this a single-step episode, which is a subset of an n-step episode. The paper that presents ER also presents a fairness-based regularization method to find solutions that mitigate ER. 

- How easy is it to find policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, as is the case in many real-world applications? (For example, in applications where one might consider race, gender, or relevant proxies.)  One of the issues with just adding a (fairness-based) penalty to the objective is that the alg is only given incentive to satisfy fairness, but has the freedom to violate fairness if that results in a more improvement to the original objective. Qualitatively, it seems that scaling the importance of each of the additional pair-wise penalties wrt the primary objective would not be simple or insightful for users. 

- Adding on to the previous point, the Zafar paper that inspired the covariance-based EI penalty creates proxies to the original fairness constraint, but does not show (theoretically) that solutions found by the new proxy-objective solve the original objective. The covariance-based EI method also has no guarantees on the solution found by the algorithms (perhaps the KDE-based penalty as well though I am unfamiliar with the work which inspired it, and did not take the time to read through the work). Ultimately this is a weakness of using fairness-based regularization to mitigate unfairness. I suggest that the users address this as a weakness in the main body.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: Overall the paper is straightforward to read. The proofs I skimmed (app A) seem clear. 
- Page 6 in experimental setting: Where are the "statistics for five trials"? Are the errors standard error or standard deviation? 

Reproducibility: Information needed for reproducibility is located in the appendix. I did not look at the code but the authors also assert that hyperparameters used in the experiments are located there as well. I feel confident that I could re-construct the experiments from the paper.

Quality/Novelty: The authors show that the notion of fairness considered is similar to current long-term metrics but distinct, and provide three algorithms, two of which are small changes from previous works that use soft constraints, i.e., create a fairness-based regularization penalty. These methods do not come with guarantees on the solution found by the optimization process, but can be considered stepping stones for future work.  

Small typos / issues and suggested improvements: 
- Appendix B first sentence: "we explain what each term means in EI definition means" -> "we explain what each term in the EI definition means" 
- I did not realize until the end of the paper that there was a second related work section---I suggest mentioning this in the first related work section! 
- "First-order" is never truly defined, and the reader is left to infer from the text. Does first-order imply that these metrics "take potential follow-up inequity risk" one "time-step" into the future, i.e., one decision into the future? The experimental section shows a one-step scenario (the first set of experiments), and a multi-step scenario (three rounds). Are the long-term metrics mentioned actually n-step fairness notions? 
- EI disparity can be inferred from the main text but I suggest that to reduce confusion it is defined in the main work. ATM the authors refer to appendix B.3 and then use this term later numerous times in the main body.

Summary Of The Review:
I make a recommendation to accept. 

- The approach seems well-motivated, and the authors take care to show that their metric is distinct from other current methods in the longterm fairness space. 
- The paper also does not over-state its claims, e.g., the three optimization algorithms introduced offer no guarantees on the solutions found will actually mitigate EI unfairness. This limitation is well-known in regards to soft constraints but is still important to address in the main body (see weaknesses for an elaboration). 
- There are also a few clarifying points from the experiments I would like addressed, such as the choice of baselines for the non-dynamic experiments, as well as the statistics being presented in the experiments. 

--- post rebuttal --- 
I thank the authors for the detailed response! The authors agreed to address many of my concerns in their next iteration, which I think will make the work more straightforward. After discussion with other reviewers, I still lean towards acceptance.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes a new fairness notion called Equal Improvability (EI) and presents three methods aimed at mitigating EI unfairness. The authors provide a clear and well-structured argument for the importance of EI as a fairness metric, and their work has some notable strengths.

One of the significant strengths of this paper is its well-written introduction and related work section. The authors effectively convey the relevance and importance of the EI metric, providing a solid foundation for their proposed methods. Additionally, the explanations and definitions provided throughout the paper are clear and concise. For instance, the example of $\mu(\Delta \mathbf{x_1})$ in Definition 2.1 helps to clarify the concept. The organization of Section 3 is straightforward, making it easy to follow the authors' reasoning.

However, there are several areas where the paper could be improved. One major weakness is the lack of comparison between other long-term fairness metrics and long-term fairness-unaware metrics in the first set of experiments. This omission makes it difficult to fully assess the effectiveness of the proposed EI metric. Furthermore, the paper does not discuss the ease of finding policies that mitigate unfairness when there are multiple groups, which is an important consideration.

Another limitation of the paper is that the fairness-based regularization methods presented do not provide guarantees on the solutions found by the algorithms. While the authors acknowledge these limitations, it would be beneficial to discuss them more thoroughly in the main body of the paper. Moreover, the definition of EI disparity could be clarified in the main text to avoid any confusion.

In terms of clarity, quality, novelty, and reproducibility, the paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The proofs in Appendix A are clear, and the appendix provides sufficient information for reproducibility. The authors also assert that the hyperparameters used in the experiments are available.

To improve the paper, I recommend addressing the following suggestions. Firstly, the authors should define EI disparity in the main text to ensure clarity. Secondly, they should mention the second related work section in the first related work section to provide a more cohesive narrative. Thirdly, the term 'first-order' and its implications should be clarified to avoid confusion. Finally, the limitations of fairness-based regularization methods should be discussed more thoroughly in the main body.

Overall, I believe that this paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, warranting a rating of 6. While it presents some interesting ideas and a clear argument for the EI metric, it requires revisions to address the identified weaknesses and suggestions. With these revisions, the paper has the potential to make a valuable contribution to the field of fairness metrics.

**Recommendation:** Accept with revisions.

**Rating:** 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the delayed impact of fairness. The authors introduce a notion of fairness called \emph{equal improvability}, which, subject to a bounded amount of improvement, equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations. The authors aim to solve the typical loss minimization problem of classification subject to the equal improbability constraint. Since the constraint is non-convex, the paper proposes 3 different approaches to solve the constrained optimization. The paper concludes with empirical analysis both in the one-shot game as well as the repeated setting.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
Strengths:
------------------------------------------
-- The delayed impact of fairness in machine learning has not received much attention in the community (mainly due to the hardness of modeling this impact) so in that sense, it is great to see papers on this topic.

--The paper is pretty well written and it is clear to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The delayed impact notion of fairness is natural and sensible.

------------------------------------------
Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
-- The main technical contribution of the paper is the definition. Hence, the technical novelty (when enforcing the equal improvability constraint) is limited. 

-- Section 4.2 contains the most interesting part of the paper, though some of the details are hard to follow/justify. It would be great to justify the choice of $\epsilon(x)$ as well as describe in more detail how the updates to the classifier are done.

------------------------------------------
Minor Comments:
------------------------------------------
-- Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 show that equal improvability and bounded effort are mathematically different. It would be nice to 
describe this in more detail as it is not clear to me how to set the reward function in Heidari et al., 2019 to connect the two works.

-- Are there deeper connections between equal recourse and equal improvability that are overlooked? If not, is not it surprising that these two methods work exactly the same in the experiments in section 4.2?

-- Please stop using the Adult dataset: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html

-- The Y value in Figure 4 should be bounded by 1. Why is it above one in the left two sub-figures?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies the delayed impact of fairness. The authors introduce a notion of fairness called \emph{equal improvability}, which, subject to a bounded amount of improvement, equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations. The authors aim to solve the typical loss minimization problem of classification subject to the equal improbability constraint. Since the constraint is non-convex, the paper proposes 3 different approaches to solve the constrained optimization. The paper concludes with empirical analysis both in the one-shot game as well as the repeated setting.

Strength And Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
Strengths:
------------------------------------------
-- The delayed impact of fairness in machine learning has not received much attention in the community (mainly due to the hardness of modeling this impact) so in that sense, it is great to see papers on this topic.

--The paper is pretty well written and it is clear to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The delayed impact notion of fairness is natural and sensible.

------------------------------------------
Weaknesses:
------------------------------------------
-- The main technical contribution of the paper is the definition. Hence, the technical novelty (when enforcing the equal improvability constraint) is limited. 

-- Section 4.2 contains the most interesting part of the paper, though some of the details are hard to follow/justify. It would be great to justify the choice of $\epsilon(x)$ as well as describe in more detail how the updates to the classifier are done.

------------------------------------------
Minor Comments:
------------------------------------------
-- Theorem 2.5 and Corollary 2.6 show that equal improvability and bounded effort are mathematically different. It would be nice to 
describe this in more detail as it is not clear to me how to set the reward function in Heidari et al., 2019 to connect the two works.

-- Are there deeper connections between equal recourse and equal improvability that are overlooked? If not, is not it surprising that these two methods work exactly the same in the experiments in section 4.2?

-- Please stop using the Adult dataset: https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2021/hash/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Abstract.html

-- The Y value in Figure 4 should be bounded by 1. Why is it above one in the left two sub-figures?

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is clear and it is easy to follow the high-level ideas of the paper. The paper is relevant to the community. The definitions are novel though the connections to prior work should be described more clearly (see above). The code is provided.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall, I enjoyed reading the paper. I think it is a nice addition to the growing literature on the delayed impact of fairness. Though the technical contributions are not strong, it can be a welcome addition to the ICLR's program.

------------------------
Post rebuttal:
------------------------
I want to sincerely thank the authors for answering my questions thoroughly. I have also read the other reviews and author responses. I think we all feel that this work can go either way. I am still (slightly) in favor of acceptance though.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper introduces a novel fairness notion called Equal Improvability (EI) and proposes three approaches to achieve EI fairness. The authors argue that the delayed impact of fairness in machine learning is an important topic that has not received sufficient attention. I find the paper to be well-written, relevant to the community, and the EI notion of fairness to be natural and sensible.

The paper's main technical contribution is the definition of EI, which, although important, limits the technical novelty. The authors propose three approaches to solve the constrained optimization problem of achieving EI fairness, but some details, particularly in Section 4.2, are hard to follow or justify. Specifically, the choice of ε(x) and the updates to the classifier are not described in sufficient detail, making it challenging to fully assess the proposed approaches.

Despite these limitations, I believe the paper has merits that make it suitable for publication. The EI notion of fairness equalizes the probability of acceptance for the rejected members across all populations subject to a bounded amount of improvement, which is a valuable contribution to the field. The paper's relevance to the community and its clear presentation also support its acceptance.

However, to strengthen the paper, I recommend that the authors provide more justification for the choice of ε(x) and describe the updates to the classifier in more detail. Additionally, clarifying the connection between EI and Bounded Effort (BE) fairness, as well as exploring deeper connections between Equal Recourse (ER) and EI, would further enhance the paper's technical contributions.

Overall, I conclude that this paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold, warranting a rating of 6. While it has some limitations in terms of technical novelty and detail, its relevance, clear presentation, and valuable contributions to the field of fairness in machine learning make it suitable for publication. I encourage the authors to address the suggested revisions to further improve the paper's quality and impact. 

Rating: 6

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a new notion of fairness called Equal Improvability (EI) that requires a binary classifier to be equally difficult to change predictions for each sensitive attribution group. To achieve this property, three different penalizations are discussed: covariance-based EI penalty, KDE-based EI penalty, and loss-based EI Penalty. The authors empirically showed that the three penalization methods achieve EI disparity. In addition, they claimed that EI promotes long-term fairness in dynamic scenarios using synthetic data.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
- Strength
  - The paper considers a timely important problem: training a fair machine learning model.

- Weaknesses
  - **Novelty:** It seems the novelty is weak. Specifically, the author proposed EI  
$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right) = \mathbb{P}\left( \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right) 
$$
which is equivalent to
$$ \mathbb{E} \left[  \mathbf{1} \set{ \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 }  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right] = \mathbb{E} \left[  \mathbf{1} \set{ \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 }  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right]. $$
Given that the same conditional distributions are considered in Equal Resource (ER) by Gupta et al. (2019), it seems the current idea might be a naive variant of the previous idea:
$$
\mathbb{E} \left[ \min_{f(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})>0.5} \mu( \mathbf{x}^\prime - \mathbf{x})  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right] = \mathbb{E} \left[  \min_{f(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})>0.5} \mu( \mathbf{x}^\prime - \mathbf{x})  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right].
$$
Also, using a fairness regularization function is not a new idea as the authors mentioned in section 3. To be fair, I believe the work can still have strengths even with these similarities. However, it is not clear why the proposed idea is powerful without comprehensive comparisons with ER. The authors explained the limitation of ER in section 2, but the behavior of EI on outliers is not explicitly examined. 

  - **Mismatch:** Mismatch between the motivation and the proposed method: the authors discussed the long-term fairness in Abstract and Introduction, but the proposed method is not directly solving the dynamics of distribution shifts. In section 4.1, the dynamic setting is considered, but it is not clear how the objective function in section 4.1.1 is related to the equation (1) in section 3.

  - **Experiments:** Most of experiments consider fairly simple settings ($\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}$, $\mathcal{Z}=\{0,1\}$, and $T$ is small) that are not realistic. The real problems should be higher dimensionality on $\mathbf{x}$ and have more sensitive classes. Moreover, it is not clear why achieving EI helps to improve long-term fairness.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a new notion of fairness called Equal Improvability (EI) that requires a binary classifier to be equally difficult to change predictions for each sensitive attribution group. To achieve this property, three different penalizations are discussed: covariance-based EI penalty, KDE-based EI penalty, and loss-based EI Penalty. The authors empirically showed that the three penalization methods achieve EI disparity. In addition, they claimed that EI promotes long-term fairness in dynamic scenarios using synthetic data.

Strength And Weaknesses:
- Strength
  - The paper considers a timely important problem: training a fair machine learning model.

- Weaknesses
  - **Novelty:** It seems the novelty is weak. Specifically, the author proposed EI  
$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right) = \mathbb{P}\left( \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right) 
$$
which is equivalent to
$$ \mathbb{E} \left[  \mathbf{1} \set{ \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 }  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right] = \mathbb{E} \left[  \mathbf{1} \set{ \max_{  \mu( \Delta \mathbf{x}_1 ) \leq \delta} f(\mathbf{x} + \Delta\mathbf{x}) > 0.5 }  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right]. $$
Given that the same conditional distributions are considered in Equal Resource (ER) by Gupta et al. (2019), it seems the current idea might be a naive variant of the previous idea:
$$
\mathbb{E} \left[ \min_{f(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})>0.5} \mu( \mathbf{x}^\prime - \mathbf{x})  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5, \mathbf{z}=z \right] = \mathbb{E} \left[  \min_{f(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})>0.5} \mu( \mathbf{x}^\prime - \mathbf{x})  \mid f(\mathbf{x}) < 0.5 \right].
$$
Also, using a fairness regularization function is not a new idea as the authors mentioned in section 3. To be fair, I believe the work can still have strengths even with these similarities. However, it is not clear why the proposed idea is powerful without comprehensive comparisons with ER. The authors explained the limitation of ER in section 2, but the behavior of EI on outliers is not explicitly examined. 

  - **Mismatch:** Mismatch between the motivation and the proposed method: the authors discussed the long-term fairness in Abstract and Introduction, but the proposed method is not directly solving the dynamics of distribution shifts. In section 4.1, the dynamic setting is considered, but it is not clear how the objective function in section 4.1.1 is related to the equation (1) in section 3.

  - **Experiments:** Most of experiments consider fairly simple settings ($\mathbf{x}_t \in \mathbb{R}$, $\mathcal{Z}=\{0,1\}$, and $T$ is small) that are not realistic. The real problems should be higher dimensionality on $\mathbf{x}$ and have more sensitive classes. Moreover, it is not clear why achieving EI helps to improve long-term fairness.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is fairly well written. 
 - The proposed methods are well explained with clear notations
 - Related works are thoroughly reviewed.

I haven't checked the details, but it seems there is no issue with reproducibility.

Summary Of The Review:
I think the main issue of the current submission is the lack of novelty and comprehensive experiments. I am happy to revise my reviews if there is anything trivial I miss something.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Peer Review**

The paper proposes a new fairness notion called Equal Improvability (EI) and discusses three penalization methods to achieve it. While the paper tackles a timely and important problem of training fair machine learning models, there are significant concerns that need to be addressed.

**Strengths:**

The paper is well-written, and the proposed methods are clearly explained with proper notations. The authors have done a thorough job in reviewing related works, providing a comprehensive background for their proposed approach. The problem of fairness in machine learning is indeed crucial, and the authors' attempt to address it is commendable.

**Weaknesses:**

However, upon closer inspection, it becomes apparent that the paper suffers from several weaknesses. Firstly, the novelty of the proposed EI notion is questionable, as it bears a strong resemblance to the Equal Resource (ER) concept introduced by Gupta et al. (2019). The authors need to provide a more convincing argument on how EI differs from ER and why it is a valuable addition to the existing literature.

Furthermore, there is a noticeable mismatch between the motivation and the proposed method. The authors emphasize the importance of addressing distribution shifts, but their proposed method does not directly tackle this issue. This disconnect raises concerns about the effectiveness of EI in achieving long-term fairness.

The experimental settings are also a major concern. Most experiments are conducted in fairly simple and unrealistic settings, which limits the generalizability of the results. The authors should strive to conduct more comprehensive experiments, including those with higher dimensionality and more sensitive classes.

**Suggestions:**

To strengthen their paper, the authors should address the following concerns:

1. Provide comprehensive comparisons with ER to demonstrate the advantages of EI.
2. Examine the behavior of EI on outliers explicitly, as this could have significant implications for fairness.
3. Conduct experiments on more realistic and complex settings to validate the effectiveness of EI.
4. Clarify how achieving EI contributes to long-term fairness, as the current motivation and method seem disconnected.

**Technical Details:**

The definition of EI, as provided, seems to rely on existing fairness regularization functions, which is not a new idea. The authors should be cautious in claiming novelty and ensure that they properly cite and acknowledge existing works.

**Overall Assessment:**

In conclusion, while the paper tackles an important problem and is well-written, it falls short in terms of novelty, experimental settings, and clarity. Given the concerns raised, I would rate this paper as marginally above the acceptance threshold (6). To improve their chances of acceptance, the authors should address the weaknesses and suggestions outlined above. With significant revisions, this paper has the potential to make a meaningful contribution to the field of fairness in machine learning.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a new notion of group-level fairness, i.e., Equal Improvability (EI). The intuition behind the notion is that, by considering those who are rejected but are not too far away from the decision border, the effort taken by those to recourse should not vary among groups. The paper provides three empirical ways to (approximately) enforce EI in classification tasks. Empirical results in the simulated dynamics are also provided.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
## Strength

The proposed EI notion combines advantages of previous notions of a similar flavor (as summarized in Table 1), and makes an effort to avoid (some) problems of previous group-level fairness notions. Three different ways to (approximately) enforce EI are also provided. The authors demonstrate the benefit of considering EI in the dynamic setting in the experiments.

## Weakness

### 1. w.r.t. the distinction between zero-order vs. first-order fairness

I am wondering what is the "order" over here. Roughly, I think the paper is pointing out the fact that EI (among others, e.g., Equal Recourse, Bounded Effort) cares about the ability to change the status quo. However, since it is a shared characteristics among some previous notions, I am not sure what specific benefit is unique to EI. Also, this distinction is only made in the beginning of the paper, and was not mentioned after Section 2. It would be very helpful if authors can share some insight over here, especially for the purpose of long-term fairness, as claimed in the paper.

### 2. the unmentioned connection between individual-level recourse fairness and EI

If I understand it correctly, the central claim w.r.t. the advantage of EI compared to other notions is the potential to "equally" improve decision outcome for those who are currently rejected. This intuition is shared by, as pointed out in the paper, effort-based fairness notions. In my opinion, the intuition is actually more aligned with individual-level recourse fairness, e.g., von Kügelgen et al. (2022) "On the Fairness of Causal Algorithmic Recourse". EI claims to be more focused on those who are potentially able to recourse. However, the fact that EI remains a group-level fairness notion indicates that individual-level recourse fairness is more fine-grained and can better serve the intended purpose laid out by the authors. Additional discussion on the connection and difference would be very helpful.

### 3. w.r.t Definition 2.1 EI

I am wondering why we should limit our consideration to $\Delta x$ such that $\Delta_M x = 0$. According to the notion, by definition mutable features do not contribute to the cost of effort but can still alter the final prediction outcome. Then, I think it is more reasonable to encode such detail in the measurement of cost instead of forcing $\Delta_M x = 0$, since this will implicitly limit the possibility to recourse for certain individuals and in turn affect the estimation of recourse probability.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Overall, the paper is not hard to follow. The results are relatively clearly presented. The paper can benefit from some additional illustrations/discussions of the claimed advantages of EI. The implementation details are provided in the paper for reproducibility.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper proposes a new group-level fairness notion and three ways to empirically enforce it in classification. There are some worries about the claimed advantages of EI, as well as the formulation of the definition of EI itself (as detailed in "Strength and Weakness"). It would be greatly appreciated if authors can kindly provide additional clarifications.

====== Post rebuttal =========

Thank authors for the response. I have updated my evaluation.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a new notion of group-level fairness, i.e., Equal Improvability (EI). The intuition behind the notion is that, by considering those who are rejected but are not too far away from the decision border, the effort taken by those to recourse should not vary among groups. The paper provides three empirical ways to (approximately) enforce EI in classification tasks. Empirical results in the simulated dynamics are also provided.

Strength And Weaknesses:
## Strength

The proposed EI notion combines advantages of previous notions of a similar flavor (as summarized in Table 1), and makes an effort to avoid (some) problems of previous group-level fairness notions. Three different ways to (approximately) enforce EI are also provided. The authors demonstrate the benefit of considering EI in the dynamic setting in the experiments.

## Weakness

### 1. w.r.t. the distinction between zero-order vs. first-order fairness

I am wondering what is the "order" over here. Roughly, I think the paper is pointing out the fact that EI (among others, e.g., Equal Recourse, Bounded Effort) cares about the ability to change the status quo. However, since it is a shared characteristics among some previous notions, I am not sure what specific benefit is unique to EI. Also, this distinction is only made in the beginning of the paper, and was not mentioned after Section 2. It would be very helpful if authors can share some insight over here, especially for the purpose of long-term fairness, as claimed in the paper.

### 2. the unmentioned connection between individual-level recourse fairness and EI

If I understand it correctly, the central claim w.r.t. the advantage of EI compared to other notions is the potential to "equally" improve decision outcome for those who are currently rejected. This intuition is shared by, as pointed out in the paper, effort-based fairness notions. In my opinion, the intuition is actually more aligned with individual-level recourse fairness, e.g., von Kügelgen et al. (2022) "On the Fairness of Causal Algorithmic Recourse". EI claims to be more focused on those who are potentially able to recourse. However, the fact that EI remains a group-level fairness notion indicates that individual-level recourse fairness is more fine-grained and can better serve the intended purpose laid out by the authors. Additional discussion on the connection and difference would be very helpful.

### 3. w.r.t Definition 2.1 EI

I am wondering why we should limit our consideration to $\Delta x$ such that $\Delta_M x = 0$. According to the notion, by definition mutable features do not contribute to the cost of effort but can still alter the final prediction outcome. Then, I think it is more reasonable to encode such detail in the measurement of cost instead of forcing $\Delta_M x = 0$, since this will implicitly limit the possibility to recourse for certain individuals and in turn affect the estimation of recourse probability.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Overall, the paper is not hard to follow. The results are relatively clearly presented. The paper can benefit from some additional illustrations/discussions of the claimed advantages of EI. The implementation details are provided in the paper for reproducibility.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a new group-level fairness notion and three ways to empirically enforce it in classification. There are some worries about the claimed advantages of EI, as well as the formulation of the definition of EI itself (as detailed in "Strength and Weakness"). It would be greatly appreciated if authors can kindly provide additional clarifications.

====== Post rebuttal =========

Thank authors for the response. I have updated my evaluation.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Weighted Ensemble Self-Supervised Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies how ensemble methods can improve self-supervised learning algorithms. The considerations are limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, to ensure no extra computational cost involved. The authors propose a kind of data-dependent weighted cross-entropy losses. Two sota SSL methods, DINO and MSN are considered as baselines. In various experimental settings, the ensemble methods are observed to largely improve the baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- This paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The ensemble methods only incur a small training cost.
- It is interesting to see that sota SSL algorithms can be further improved by ensembling in various settings (linear eval, KNN, few-shot, etc.).

Weaknesses:
- The proposed framework only suits contrastive SSL methods (i.e., align positive pairs). Other SSL methods such as MAE can not be applied.
- When encoders are not considered for the ensemble, the possibilities for where to ensemble are not too many. The proposed ensemble framework somewhat lacks novelty. The main contribution is to test different weighting schemes.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
- Clarity: This paper is well-organized and easy to read.
- Novelty: The proposed ensemble framework seems to be a little bit simple. But the experiments are sufficient.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall,  the problem this paper studies is interesting. To simplify the design and retain the computational cost, this paper is limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, which limits the technical novelty. It is interesting to see the significant improvement in performance under various settings and evaluation metrics. Therefore, I think this is a borderline paper.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies how ensemble methods can improve self-supervised learning algorithms. The considerations are limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, to ensure no extra computational cost involved. The authors propose a kind of data-dependent weighted cross-entropy losses. Two sota SSL methods, DINO and MSN are considered as baselines. In various experimental settings, the ensemble methods are observed to largely improve the baselines.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strength:
- This paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The ensemble methods only incur a small training cost.
- It is interesting to see that sota SSL algorithms can be further improved by ensembling in various settings (linear eval, KNN, few-shot, etc.).

Weaknesses:
- The proposed framework only suits contrastive SSL methods (i.e., align positive pairs). Other SSL methods such as MAE can not be applied.
- When encoders are not considered for the ensemble, the possibilities for where to ensemble are not too many. The proposed ensemble framework somewhat lacks novelty. The main contribution is to test different weighting schemes.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
- Clarity: This paper is well-organized and easy to read.
- Novelty: The proposed ensemble framework seems to be a little bit simple. But the experiments are sufficient.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall,  the problem this paper studies is interesting. To simplify the design and retain the computational cost, this paper is limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, which limits the technical novelty. It is interesting to see the significant improvement in performance under various settings and evaluation metrics. Therefore, I think this is a borderline paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The paper presents a weighted ensemble self-supervised learning method that improves the performance of various SSL techniques, including DINO and MSN, by ensembling projection heads and/or codebooks. The proposed method utilizes a family of weighted cross-entropy losses, grounded in maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) intuition, to facilitate the learning of a single representation encoder. The use of data-dependent importance weights allows for preferential treatment of some teacher/student pairs, enhancing the ensemble's diversity and performance.

The strengths of this work lie in its efficiency and tailored design for SSL, which incurs no additional cost during downstream evaluation. The flexibility of the proposed weighted ensemble losses is also noteworthy, as they accommodate various weighting schemes. The method achieves state-of-the-art results on ImageNet-1K, particularly in few-shot settings, outperforming non-ensembled baselines with a smaller number of parameters. Furthermore, the authors provide a thorough analysis of the impact of different weighting schemes on ensemble diversity and performance, shedding light on the critical role of weighting scheme choice in representation performance.

However, the method is not without its weaknesses. The choice of weighting scheme is crucial, and careful tuning of hyperparameters, such as the temperature for entropy weighting, is required. This sensitivity to hyperparameter tuning may limit the method's applicability and robustness across different datasets and tasks.

To further enhance this work, the authors could explore alternative weighting schemes, such as those that favor disagreeing teacher/student pairs, to potentially improve ensemble diversity. Additionally, analyzing the effect of different projection head architectures on the performance of the ensembled method could provide valuable insights. Investigating the applicability of the method to other SSL techniques beyond DINO and MSN would also be beneficial.

The technical details presented in the paper, such as the use of a temperature parameter to scale the entropy of teacher predictions and the employment of a smaller projection head (3-layer MLP with hidden size 1024) to improve few-shot evaluation performance, demonstrate a thorough understanding of the proposed method's intricacies.

Overall, the paper presents a well-designed and effective weighted ensemble self-supervised learning method. While there are areas for further improvement and exploration, the work's strengths and contributions to the field justify its acceptance. I recommend that this paper be accepted, as it is marginally above the acceptance threshold, with a rating of 6.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed framework only suits contrastive SSL methods (i.e., align positive pairs). Other SSL methods such as MAE can not be applied.
- When encoders are not considered for the ensemble, the possibilities for where to ensemble are not too many. The proposed ensemble framework somewhat lacks novelty. The main contribution is to test different weighting schemes.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
- Clarity: This paper is well-organized and easy to read.
- Novelty: The proposed ensemble framework seems to be a little bit simple. But the experiments are sufficient.

Summary Of The Review:
Overall,  the problem this paper studies is interesting. To simplify the design and retain the computational cost, this paper is limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, which limits the technical novelty. It is interesting to see the significant improvement in performance under various settings and evaluation metrics. Therefore, I think this is a borderline paper.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
